blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: Use of genetic algorithms to group students of engineering. There have been previous utilizations of such a methodology to create groups in lab assignments or other parts involving collaborative learning.

blocks.text: Authors evaluate the impact of their approach about the impact on collaborative performance, processes and perceptions. REsults show that there is a positive impact and that students tend to see this approach as “fair”.

blocks.text: Introduction

text.content: Introduction

rich_text.plain_text: Introduction

blocks.text: Student grouping should incorporate a multi-variate and multi-faceted approach. Students have their individual technical capabilities but other traits such as motivation to learn and/or character development should also be considered. This brings us into 2 distinct forms of grouping students:

Students have their individual technical capabilities but other traits such as motivation to learn and/or character development should also be considered. This brings us into 2 distinct forms of grouping students:

blocks.text: Homogenous based on specific criteria:

text.content: Homogenous based on specific criteria:

rich_text.plain_text: Homogenous based on specific criteria:

blocks.text: Heterogenous grouping based on multiple criteria. The latter option has been argued to bring many advantages - opportunities for mutual teaching and support, enhancing relationships and easier classroom management. However, this type of grouping is often influenced by other constraints.

blocks.text: It is important to have good classes in order to avoid situations where the best students are all bunched up into a class or another that cannot progress as well as others because it lacks “someone at the level” which can make the gaps between different classes become increasingly larger. The authors then transition into associating genetic algorithms with the constructivist perspective of learning (which I won’t deny, is definitely odd). Regardless, the paper aims to use genetic algorithms to create distinct classes that are:

blocks.text: Explanation of what GAs are follows:

text.content: Explanation of what GAs are follows:

rich_text.plain_text: Explanation of what GAs are follows:

blocks.text: Start with an initial population of students, each with its own fitness value. The individuals with higher fitness tend to be selected first and the fitness function can be understood as a measure of chromosomal compatibility. Then, there is cross-over between different chromosomes and mutations. Groups with higher fitness values tend to be selected for following iterations.

blocks.text: Objective of the paper is to make better classes with GA. Better classes in this context refers to creating classes that enhance academic achievement, promote participation and engagement and foster diversity within groups.

blocks.text: Methods

text.content: Methods

rich_text.plain_text: Methods

blocks.text: Definition of population: students will be selected from a random study programme - in this case Computer Engineering with 114 students. Students will be classified according to 11 criteria: Entrance, School of Origin, Gender, IPA value, IPS score, Language Score, Math Score, GPA, hometown, religion and Parent’s Income.

text.content: Definition of population: students will be selected from a random study programme - in this case Computer Engineering with 114 students. Students will be classified according to 11 criteria: Entrance, School of Origin, Gender, IPA value, IPS score, Language Score, Math Score, GPA, hometown, religion and Parent’s Income.

blocks.text: Genetic Algorithm: Big initial blunder mentioning that Darwin discovered Genetic Algorithms. But in general, Genetic algorithms have different individuals be a potential solution and these individuals are continuously crossed with one another until we get new individuals and new individuals. Each individual then represents a potential solution.

text.content: Genetic Algorithm: Big initial blunder mentioning that Darwin discovered Genetic Algorithms. But in general, Genetic algorithms have different individuals be a potential solution and these individuals are continuously crossed with one another until we get new individuals and new individuals. Each individual then represents a potential solution.

rich_text.plain_text: Genetic Algorithm: Big initial blunder mentioning that Darwin discovered Genetic Algorithms. But in general, Genetic algorithms have different individuals be a potential solution and these individuals are continuously crossed with one another until we get new individuals and new individuals. Each individual then represents a potential solution.

blocks.text: Other studies have already used GAs in education. Moreno used them to create well-balanced student groups according to academic performance. The fitness function for that test was an ANOVA - a statistical method meant to compare means between groups. The findings suggest that GA was better than the remaining methods at generating wel-balanced groups ( so, minimizing between group differences??).

text.content: Other studies have already used GAs in education. Moreno used them to create well-balanced student groups according to academic performance. blocks.text: Set parameters: Population size (100), Generations (50), Crossover Probability (0.3), Mutation Probability (0.1), Selection Method - Tournament Selection, Fitness Function: unspecified at this stage but meant to represent a numerical score bnased on the individual’s performance, Cross-over is single operator and mutation is bit-flip.

text.content: Implementation Steps

blocks.text: Generate 100 random individuals. Each gene refers to the criteria possessed by the students individidual that is part of the class whereas each chromossome refers to the combination of individuals to be used - lenght of 16.

text.content: Fitness Value

rich_text.plain_text: Fitness Value

blocks.text: Fitness is going to be computed as a measure of diversity. The higher the diversity in fitness values, the “more fit a chromosome” is going to be. Thus, the fitness function can be understood as a “count of unique genes”.

text.content: Fitness is going to be computed as a measure of diversity. The higher the diversity in fitness values, the “more fit a chromosome” is going to be. Thus, the fitness function can be understood as a “count of unique genes”.

blocks.text: Selection involves hill climbing - individuals with the highest fitness are kept.

blocks.text: The students were divided into 12 classes of 16 students each → problem formulation. These students are assigned randomly before the start. From this random assignment, the total fitness of this solution is 703.

blocks.text: The process, involves assigning a class code to each individual. Then, there is a score called ATT that is computed as a measure of uniqueness of the individual. The fitness function is the count of this ATT. Authors employ crossover to prevent premature convergence.

blocks.text: Final solution achieves a Fitness value of 701. When comparing this solution against that of a k-means clustering, the authors argue that there is a lot more diversity on the grouping done by genetic algorithms in comparison with the one obtained via k-means (I’ll be honest, I don’t understand how you can look at these graphs and conclude anything, but ok).
